summary:
The Mau Mau Uprising in Kenya (1952–1960) was a revolt by members of the Kikuyu ethnic group and others against British colonial rule. The movement, formally known as the Kenya Land and Freedom Army, arose primarily from grievances over land dispossession, racial discrimination, and limited economic and political opportunities for Africans under colonial rule. White settlers had occupied fertile highland areas historically inhabited by the Kikuyu, and landlessness was widespread among the African population.

The British colonial government declared a state of emergency in October 1952 and conducted a counterinsurgency campaign of considerable brutality. Over 150,000 Kikuyu were detained without trial in a network of camps where torture and abuse were systematic. British and Kenyan government forces killed between 11,000 and 20,000 Mau Mau fighters and suspected supporters. The uprising did not militarily defeat the British but contributed to the political calculation that the cost of maintaining colonial rule in Kenya was too high. Kenya gained independence in 1963 under Jomo Kenyatta, who had been imprisoned during the emergency. The British government acknowledged and apologized for the systematic abuse of detainees in 2013 and provided compensation payments.
